responseSet.title: What Does the Physicist Know?: Thraldom and Insecurity in the Relationship of Psychoanalysis to Physics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Does the Physicist Know?: Thraldom and Insecurity in the Relationship of Psychoanalysis to Physics

Joseph Schwartz PHD

Joseph Schwartz

2 Lancaster Drive
London NW3 #4HA England

Joseph Schwartz  

An unstated assumption in the important discussion What Does the Analyst Know? (Symposium, 1992, 1993) is that the questions of what is truth, of what it means to know, of what constitutes effective understandings of human experience have been resolved in the natural sciences, particularly physics. What is problematic about certain aspects of the discussion is an invidious acceptance of the validity of a positivist definition of physical science, an acceptance that places psychoanalysis as an inferior discipline to physics, as a discipline for which one must make excuses. Missing from the debate is a more textured, critical, and realistic view of the practice of physical science that can ameliorate the present insecurity and thraldom that I believe characterizes the attitudes of psychoanalysts and psychotherapists toward natural science.

By examining three vignettes, from physics, molecular biology, and psychoanalysis respectively, I hope to show that knowledge in the physical sciences is as socially constructed as it is in psychoanalysis and that just as the historicity of living matter is not a problem for biology but defines the object of study, so too is subjectivity not a problem for psychoanalysis but defines its object of study. I explore the function that the fantasy of an unproblematic natural science has served in psychoanalysis and suggest that one consequence for psychoanalysis of its failure to understand the actual practice of natural science has been a failure to take seriously and to develop with confidence what is truly original in the discipline.

Joseph Schwartz was a physicist before becoming a clinician. His latest book is a history of psychoanalysis, soon to be published.

I wish to thank Susie Orbach for her insistence that I write this paper and for her thoughtful critical readings of its numerous drafts. I also wish to thank the members of my seminar group Sally Berry, Margaret Green, Susie Orbach, and Tom Ryan for invaluable discussions of themes related to this paper.

[[RunningHead]]

Theory's Shadow: Response to John A. Lindon**

Virginia Goldner PHD

Virginia Goldner

102 East 22nd Street, #10H
New York, NY 10010

Virginia Goldner  

I am sorry that John Lindon found nothing of value in my discussion (Goldner, 1994) of his paper, “Gratification and Provision in Psychoanalysis” (Lindon, 1994). While he seems to view my choice to appreciate his work as well as to critique it as some kind of a ploy, I actually find the issues raised by his paper, and his response to my discussion of it, significant enough to warrant another foray into print.

Since our dialogue has been plagued by the mutual experience of being (willfully?) misunderstood, it raises important questions about whether, and how, discussions are to be conducted across the diverse views that characterize contemporary psychoanalytic discourse. Dialogue is, of course, the founding premise of this journal, and it is unfortunate that the conversation in question provides such a clear illustration of the “intradisciplinary communication problems” that Mitchell (1991, p. 3) articulates in the founding issue of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

In a carefully worded observation that is a model of editorial understatement, Mitchell writes that

when psychoanalysts of different persuasions attempt to talk to each other about matters of common concern … the results are often far from satisfying…. [This is because] analysts cluster in groups identified with a particular tradition, each with its own favorite words and phrases…. [which] carry with [them] many assumptions with which [those outside that tradition] may not agree [pp. 1-2].

*Psychoanalytic Dialogues 4(4), 1994.

Dr. Goldner is on the Editorial Board of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and is an Associate Editor of Gender and Psychoanalysis. She is also on the Senior Faculty of the Ackerman Institute for Family Therapy.

Eliza Redux: Commentary on Gerhardt and Stinson

Carol Feldman PHD

Carol Feldman

200 Mercer Street
New York, NY 10012

Carol Feldman  

1

Gerhardt and stinson have written a difficult and thought-provoking paper that seems to make the innocent proposal that psychoanalytic theory should be enriched by incorporating discourse-analytic notions into theory and practice. The proposal receives an interesting and sufficient theoretical justification as a way of making good the promise in the new social constructivism to give a more fleshed out characterization of the contribution of the analyst to the analytic process. But then something goes terribly wrong in the way discourse ideas are applied, that is, as universal rules regulating every patient, in every analytic dialogue, in the same way. The upshot is that, when we turn to the illustrative case study, the patient's individual way of handling the discourse situation with her analyst is seen as merely instantiating the rules and, therefore, as telling us nothing about her particular way of being in therapy or in the world.

By adopting a theory that blindfolds them to potential information about the patient's way of coping in the therapeutic dialogue, the authors are left with slim pickings when they try to interpret the case—an unweighted summary list of everything she said she felt. A most unsatisfactory interpretation results: they reassure us that, with more

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Plea for a Measure of Analytic Objectivity: Commentary on Gerhardt and Stinson

Donald Kaplan PHD

Donald Kaplan

175 West 12th Street
New York, NY 10011

Donald Kaplan  

I am happy to say that what i have come to dread these days from a paper whose title (or subtitle) contains the phrase “analytic subjectivity” did not materialize. On the contrary, I was hugely heartened by Gerhard and Stinson's engrossing paper, which recovers a number of crucial psychoanalytic tenets that have been provisionally buried in a current riot of innovation on the psychoanalytic scene.

One of the points Gerhard and Stinson are making in this intelligent and edifying paper is that it is not only possible but also desirable to be objective about another's subjectivity, and they go on to construct a sensible and not unfamiliar means for achieving this. Like subjectivity, objectivity is a feasible state of mind that can be brought about by particular conditions. This is not news. However, a lot of obfuscation about the matter still lingers on, so that the authors' arguments are no less urgent these days than they would have been formerly. In a paper of mine that goes back over 20 years (Kaplan, 1973), I coined the term “procedural instrumentation” for such conditions and quoted a passage from a paper by G. G. Simpson on the problem of objectivity in situations of which we are a part.

The passage is worth repeating:

To say that we cannot learn anything materially factual about a situation if we ourselves are in it is utter and nonsensical negation of the very meaning of learning. The essential in objectivity is not the pretense of eliminating ourselves from a situation in which we are objectively present. It is that the situation should not be interpreted in terms of ourselves but that our roles should be interpreted realistically in terms of the situation [p. 33].

Dr. Kaplan died on Sept. 20, 1994. He was Clinical Professor at New York University's Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

It Ain't Over till It's Over Response to Editors' Follow-Up Questions

Michael Basch MD

Michael Basch

55 East Washington, #3605
Chicago, IL 60602

Michael Basch  

The editors are a bit more sanguine than I am about the idea that the drive theory framework is fading. It is true that since most psychoanalysts, and certainly most psychotherapists, never read Freud in any depth, they easily dismiss drive theory as being, at best, a metaphor—it saves a lot of reading that would otherwise have to be done. The attitude of the analyst/therapist toward patients is, however, still formed by this supposedly discarded concept, although those who use it usually do not know where it comes from. One of the few people who understands drive theory and adheres to it in practice is Charles Brenner. Although his traditional position is today often criticized, in a limited number of cases it makes good sense. In a neurotic transference to the analyst, everything that the analyst does or does not do has symbolic significance and, in the interest of mobilizing the unconscious, there should be as little opportunity for the diversion of the unconscious sexual and aggressive longings that the neurotic patient has repressed. For that reason, when analyzing the psychoneurotic patient, one should eschew many of the social amenities, small kindnesses, clarifications, and helpful comments and interventions that the patient asks for directly or indirectly. That this position, appropriate for the psychoanalysis of the psychoneurotic patient, has been carried over into analytic and psychotherapeutic practice—usually without any justification other than the mistaken notion that this is what Freud dictated—is a disgrace. So, even though many agree that drive theory, from not only the psychoanalytic but also the biological point of view, has had its day and at best needs to be significantly modified in order to be useful, I still often encounter the kind of situation that happened to me recently when a young therapist in the elevator asked me if he might have a consultation with me.

[[RunningHead]]

Oedipus and Beyond: A Clinical Theory by Jay Greenberg

(Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1991, x + 274 pp.)

Leston Havens MD

Leston Havens

151 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138

Leston Havens  

All of us owe a great debt to Jay Greenberg and his colleague Stephen Mitchell for the education they have given us in their joint and respective books. Perhaps because of the very richness of this education, this review, critique, and development of analytic theories brought forcibly to my mind related questions: What is the effect of these many ideas, representations, and rerepresentations of ideas on our relationships with each other and with the patients? How do they affect our capacity to get through to one another? Are we inflating the importance of theory as we once did our treatment expectations? Are there other ways we might go about the work without losing the dignity and self-consciousness so admirable in psychoanalysis?

The histories of both medicine and psychology are replete with periods of theoretical inflation succeeded by returns to simpler questions.

Doctrinal disputes are common to every field of inquiry. My impression is they are fiercer in those areas where secure findings are most difficult to reach. I am told no field surpasses cosmology in the ferocity of its debates. There, as in psychoanalysis, large stretches of events can be only a little and occasionally sampled. Surely uncertainty drives the need to conclude, to find a resting point, however brief, from the confusion that prevails. And when we deal with often unique historical configurations, uncertainty can be impenetrable. We need both to find a path and to tolerate what may be not only uncertain but novel and emerging.

Dr. Havens is Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School, and Director of Adult Residency, Cambridge (MA) Hospital.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Locating Erikson in Contemporary Psychoanalysis: Commentary on Seligman and Shanok

Robert Wallerstein MD

Robert Wallerstein

655 Redwood Highway, #261
Mill Valley, CA 94941

Robert Wallerstein  

In a presentation on erikson's identity concept at a panel at the December 1994 meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association devoted to a reevaluation of Erikson's life work, I tried to clarify the paradox that I feel his presence in our ranks has always posed for us. In his lifetime Erikson was undoubtedly the psychoanalyst best known, most deeply esteemed, and most widely influential in the socio-historical surround of the whole world culture. His books, particularly the first, seminal Childhood and Society (Erikson, 1950), but also his Luther (1958) and Gandhi (1969) biographies, and from amongst all the others, his Identity: Youth and Crisis (1968), have, from their publication and continuing essentially unabated today, been core curricula in university courses on human development, on the life cycle, and on psychological perspectives in history and biography across the nation. And during the student rebellions of the 60s, Erikson became, unasked, a rallying cry, almost an icon, and very much a cult figure of almost mythic proportions.

Much of the contextual material of this commentary has been adapted from a panel presentation, “Erikson's Concept of Ego Identity Reconsidered: 1994,” at the American Psychoanalytic Association meeting, December 17, 1994, New York City.

Dr. Wallerstein is Professor Emeritus of Psychiatry at the University of California at San Francisco School of Medicine and a Training and Supervising Analyst at the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute. He is a Past President of the American Psychoanalytic Association (1971-72) and a Past President of the International Psychoanalytical Association (1985-89).

© 1995 The Analytic Press

Psychoanalytic Theories, Subjective Experience, and Clinical Perspectives: Reply to Wallerstein, Sander, and Altman

Stephen Seligman DMH and Rebecca Shanok MSW

Rebecca Shanok

3667 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

246 West End Avenue, #11C
New York, NY 10023

Rebecca Shanok  

We are grateful to drs. wallerstein, sander, and altman for their responses to our paper. Each emphasizes a different strand in our work: Wallerstein recognizes the integrative potential of our effort to bring Erikson's concept of identity in line with current developments in two-person psychoanalysis, and, by providing rich historical and conceptual detail, he highlights our admiration for Erikson's long-neglected but potent vision. Sander contributes his extraordinary perspective on the organization and development of personality in responding to our interest in that topic. And Altman critically confronts our advocacy of an integrative synthesis to break down what we regard as an unnecessary segregation of the established perspectives.

Wallerstein: On Paying Homage to Erikson in the Intersubjective-Object Relational Context

Wallerstein makes several points as he locates our rereading of Erikson in the context of the evolution of psychoanalytic theory. Accurately, he sees us as paying homage to Erikson at the same time as we modify his theory. Like us, Wallerstein thinks that Erikson never quite breaks through the one-person psychology of his day, although he often takes positions that represent a more radical departure, especially in fully locating the psychoanalytic subject in the social world.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Symposium on Psychoanalysis and Linguistics: Part 1

Adrienne Harris PHD

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, 5th floor
New York, NY 10003

Adrienne Harris  

In this collection of papers, published in two parts (the first in this issue, the second in the next), Psychoanalytic Dialogues considers the power, scope, and place of language process and linguistic analysis in psychoanalytic theory and practice. You might say the journal is finding, or refinding, meanings of a central element in its name—Dialogues.

Language has, of course, never been far from the epicenter of psychoanalysis: “the talking cure,” an analysand's first term for psychoanalytic process; the mutative power of remembering in words, freeing the patient from the grip of unknowing action; the rule of free association, with its powerful dependence on basic ideas of meaning and language structure; the particular shape of memory as it distorts along the fault lines of language. Theories of language and speech have been central to psychoanalysis throughout its development.

There are two focal papers in this symposium: by Gerhardt and Stinson and by Perez Foster. Although quite different in focus and content, they do carry an agenda: to bring linguistic theory in regard to bilingualism, representation, and code switching (in the case of Perez Foster) and discourse analysis and speech pragmatics (in the case of Gerhardt and Stinson) into psychoanalysts' working understanding.

In Part 1, in this issue, Gerhardt and Stinson ask us to notice that there are unobtrusive but powerful features, “demand characteristics,” embedded in the context and framework of analysis that enter and interact and coconstruct dialogue. Their work reveals one mode through which intrapsychic life is manifest in the forms of verbal interaction.

responseSet.title: The Dynamic Self in Psychoanalytic Psychology A Formulation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dynamic Self in Psychoanalytic Psychology A Formulation

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

337 Lake Park Lane
Ann Arbor, MI 48103

Irene Fast  

Following Hartmann's theoretical formulations, the self has been explored primarily as a body of self-representations coordinate to object representations; the dynamic organizations of the mind (the id-ego-superego systems) have been seen as impersonal (nonself) functions.

This paper attempts a conception of the id-ego-superego structures as self-functions in intimate relation to self-representations. Building on an integration of Piagetian and psychoanalytic object relations frameworks (event theory), it proposes that the earliest self-experience occurs in dynamic schemes of personally motivated interaction between self and nonself. These I-schemes lay the groundwork for both the dynamic and the representational aspects of the self: Their structure results in experience characteristic of the dynamic id and in their character as undifferentiated interactions provides the base for the development of self- and object representations. Development occurs by integration and differentiation in processes of conflict resolution. The paper traces these developments in outline as they lead to the transition in the dynamic self from id to ego organizations, and in the self-representations from part-selves and part-objects to a whole and individuated self in relation to whole objects.

responseSet.title: Analysis of a Case of Transsexualism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analysis of a Case of Transsexualism

Ruth Stein PHD

Ruth Stein

25 Tchernichovski Street
Jerusalem 92578
Israel

Ruth Stein  

Excerpts from prolonged analytic work with a multiply traumatized young man who deliberated a sex-change operation are presented, highlighting some problematics in the treatment of transsexualism, a gender-identity disturbance that may become significant in recent psychoanalytic and cultural discourse on self and gender. The paper aims both to report some “technical” issues in such an analysis and to throw some light on such themes as identification, domination and submission, sadomasochism and guilt and shame, narcissistic entitlement and empowerment, desire and recognition, gender and difference, and the vertiginously multiple meanings and emotional elaborations of masculinity and femininity. The analytic itinerary is recounted with emphasis on diverse relational approaches that were attempted to fit the delicate balance of the patient's needs, his quasi-delusional transferences, and his capacity to persevere in a mutually “nightmarish,” though possible and feasible psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Intersubjective View of Self Psychology

Robert Stolorow PHD

Robert Stolorow

11726 San Vicente Boulevard, # 410
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Robert Stolorow  

This article locates self psychology within the context of the evolving paradigm for psychoanalysis that the author calls intersubjectivity theory. It is argued that Kohut contributed significantly to the new paradigm but stopped short of fully embracing it. Self psychology and intersubjectivity theory are compared and contrasted, and an intersubjective view is offered of the patient's transference, the analyst's transference, and the system created by their reciprocal interaction.

responseSet.title: The Internalized Primal Scene
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Internalized Primal Scene

Lewis Aron PHD

Lewis Aron

243 West End Avenue, #310
New York, NY 10023

Lewis Aron  

An important critique has been developing that questions the ways in which psychoanalytic theories have conceptualized gender and sexuality. Contemporary (postmodern) feminist theories and the work of gay and lesbian theorists have challenged the traditional psychoanalytic view of gender as formed primarily through fixed, unitary identifications. This paper reviews the classical concept of the primal scene and the Kleinian concept of the combined parent figure and recontextualizes these ideas within contemporary relational theory. The revised metaphor of an internalized primal scene challenges traditional notions of a unitary gender identity and lends support to the postmodern critique of the notion of a core or unified identity. This paper develops the idea that we need both a notion of gender identity and a notion of gender multiplicity; more broadly, we need an emphasis on people both as unified, stable, cohesive subjects and as multiple, fragmented, and different from moment to moment. In line with the postmodern emphasis on deconstructing dichotomies, the paper emphasizes the deconstruction, or rather the psychoanalysis, of such polarized concepts as male-female, masculine—feminine, heterosexual—homosexual, father—mother, genital—pregenital, oedipal—preoedipal, identity—multiplicity, identity—multiplicitly paranoid—schizoid—depressive position, drive theory—relational theory, and even patient—analyst.

An earlier version of this paper was presented at the spring meeting of the Division of Psychoanalysis (39) of the American Psychological Association, April 12, 1991. I am indebted to many colleagues who read drafts of this paper and gave helpful criticism. I especially want to thank Muriel Dimen, whose clarity and insight into the very complex area of gender and postmodern feminist critique were invaluable. Thanks also to Jessica Benjamin, Virginia Goldner, Adrienne Harris, Joyce McDougall, Stephen Mitchell, Gilead Nachmani, Thomas Ogden, David Schwartz, Elizabeth Spillius, and Carole Vance.

Sadly, my revered teacher, supervisor, and colleague, Donald Kaplan, did not live to see the final version of this paper. He had read and commented on two earlier drafts, and although we often disagreed he enthusiastically encouraged my scholarship. I dedicate this paper to his memory.

Dr. Aron is Clinical Associate Professor and Supervisor, New York University Post-doctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, and Clinical Associate Professor and Supervisor, Derner Institute Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, Adelphi University.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Identity and the Experience of Specificity in a Process of Recognition: Commentary on Seligman and Shanok
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Identity and the Experience of Specificity in a Process of Recognition: Commentary on Seligman and Shanok

Louis Sander MD

Louis Sander

2525 Madrona Avenue
St. Helena, CA 94574

Louis Sander  

In this timely and detailed effort to explicate and enlarge upon the enduring contribution to the “problem of identity” introduced, some 45 years ago, by Erik Erikson (1950), the authors seek to craft an even wider synthesis, bringing together a broad inclusiveness of perspectives that offers a way of integrating the classical psychoanalytic with the more recent object relations, self-psychological, and, especially, systems viewpoints. At least three main statements seem to emerge from the complexity of the paper: The first is that “identity” must be thought of as an ongoing, lifelong, integrative process, not as a feature having the static fixity of structure nor as a developmental outcome of childhood; the second is that “self” must be conceived of as being held within the actual exchanges with one's ongoing personal-social-historical context, not apart from it; and third, the process within which the unity of person seeks to organize its coherence involves the continuing negotiation of a complex array of dialectic tensions among numerous polarities inherent in the constraints and components that constitute the system.

It is possible, within the limits of this discussion, to address only two features of this complex paper, in which the significance of the authors' message can become obscured by the breadth of the synthesis they attempt. The first concerns the matter of inclusiveness itself, both its scope and its significance.

Louis W. Sander, M.D. is Visiting Professor of Psychiatry, Boston University, School of Medicine and Professor of Psychiatry Emeritus, University of Colorado Medical School.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Melancholy Gender—Refused Identification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melancholy Gender—Refused Identification

Judith Butler PHD

Judith Butler

Dept. of Rhetoric
University of California|
Berkeley, CA 94720

Judith Butler  

Freud's discussion of melancholy in “Mourning and Melancholia” includes an account of identification as the incorporation of the lost object. This essay first seeks to establish a relation between that incorporative identification and the formation of “the bodily ego.” It then seeks to situate this melancholic condition of the bodily ego in terms of the “loss” of the same-sexed object under prevalent conditions of compulsory heterosexuality. This “loss” might be better understood on the model of foreclosure, suggesting that it is a loss resolved into a melancholic identification and hence central to the formation of same-sex gender identification. This account of the melancholic consequences of a disavowed homosexual attachment is then situated in terms of contemporary conditions of grief over the loss by aids of so many gay men. The suggestion here is that the cultural “unreality” of that “loss” may be attributable to the foreclosed status of homosexual love as that which “never was” and “never was lost.”

responseSet.title: Multiplicities and Uncertainties of Gender: Commentary on Ruth Stein's “Analysis of a Case of Transsexualism”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Multiplicities and Uncertainties of Gender: Commentary on Ruth Stein's “Analysis of a Case of Transsexualism”

Nancy Chodorow PHD

Nancy Chodorow

5305 College Avenue
Oakland, CA 94618

Nancy Chodorow  

Ruth Stein's “analysis of a case of transsexualism” represents almost perfectly where we (or many of us) have arrived in our attempts to understand and analyze gender. In what follows, I offer some reflections about where this is.

To begin, we need to be grateful to Dr. Stein for putting before us the details of an analysis, including her own stumblings, rethinkings, frustrations, and hopes. Without such details—all too often missing in our literature or replaced by short “vignettes” obligatorily added to more theoretical pieces in such a way that there is not enough material for readers to form their own opinions or to disagree with what the author claims the vignette “confirms”—we are really unable to assess whether our theoretical advances make empirical sense and whether they help our clinical work.

Where we find ourselves, and especially where readers of this journal find themselves (see esp. Dimen, 1991; Goldner, 1991; and Harris, 1991), is at the point of having concluded that gendered subjectivity, gender identity, and gender-inflected sexuality are personally constructed and variable. Among individuals, and even for a single individual, gender seems to have multiple meanings: maddeningly, these meanings do not peel off like the proverbial onion as we analyze, one by one, the defensive layers that construct them, leaving, finally, as Freud claimed (and probably hoped), bedrock.

Nancy J. Chodorow, Ph.D. is Member and Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Professor of Sociology, University of California, Berkeley; and author of The Reproduction of Mothering (1978), Feminism and Psychoanalytic Theory (1989), and Femininities, Masculinities, Sexualities (1994).

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: “Successful” Dissociation, Pseudovitality, and Inauthentic Use of the Senses
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Successful” Dissociation, Pseudovitality, and Inauthentic Use of the Senses

Peter Goldberg PHD

Peter Goldberg

1562 Oakview Avenue
Kensington, CA 94707

Peter Goldberg  

Dissociation is posited as a form of psychical organization in which psychical conflicts and threats to self-preservation are regulated on the terrain of mind/body relations. Economic, social, and cultural processes are implicated in the establishment of stable forms of dissociation, and at the same time render these dissociative regimes opaque. This essay attempts to show 1) how the dissociative organization and process relies on a mechanism that may be described as a type of de-repression; 2) that dissociative communication is not symbolic in nature but employs a signaling function, giving rise to pseudointegration of the personality rather than true integration; 3) how a phenomenon of false encoding may be observed in these cases; and 4) how a stable regime of pathological dissociation subtly creates a sensory cocoon or invisible wall, the effect of which is to erect a narcissistic field of omnipotent constructions (a “pathological container”) in the place of actual contact between people.

Lastly, it is in the structure of pathological dissociation that it is possible to locate a specific psychological definition of inauthenticity.

Hope as Inspiration in Psychoanalysis

Sandra Buechler PHD

Sandra Buechler

27 West 72nd Street, #807
New York, NY 10023

Sandra Buechler  

In this paper, hope is explored as a motivating force in analysis. To see the patient's and the analyst's hopes in terms of changes they expect the treatment to accomplish emphasizes the cognitive aspect of hope. While touching on these cognitive expectations, this paper focuses on the emotional, rather than the cognitive, function of hope in treatment. It addresses the question of how hope can inspire analytic participants to have the strength and stamina that analysis requires.

responseSet.title: Interaction in the Psychoanalytic Situation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interaction in the Psychoanalytic Situation

Emmanuel Ghent MD

Emmanuel Ghent

Emmanuel Ghent  

In psychoanalytic psychotherapy we are primarily interested in psychic change and how to facilitate it for the better. Change is a universal property of matter, living or inanimate: everything in nature is influenced by everything else; interaction is ubiquitous. In the early years of psychoanalysis, the prevailing view was that therapeusis was essentially informational—insight and awareness would bring about changes in the ways one would experience events and respond to them. Over time, there has been a subtle shift from the informational perspective to the transformational, where insight is often retrospective rather than the active agent. The growing awareness of the need to be deeply recognized and responded to by another human being is reflective of this shift and has loomed ever larger in the interactive arena known as psychoanalysis. This paper focuses on a single facet of the need for recognition by another—the role of enacted response in effecting psychic change. It also addresses another level of the meaning of interaction: Whereas the internal interaction between perception and response has tended to be looked upon, in psychoanalysis, as unidirectional, the present discussion draws attention to the complexity of the bidirectional interaction between perception and response.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self Psychology: Today

Frank Lachmann PHD and Beatrice Beebe PHD

Beatrice Beebe

393 West End Avenue, #1-A
New York, NY 10024

722 W. 168th Street, #108
New York, NY 10032

Beatrice Beebe  

Self psychology is maintaining continuity with Kohut's last work, How Does Analysis Cure?, and his vision of human nature, as well as exploring a multiplicity of new directions. This paper discusses the expansion of Kohut's contributions in terms of the “figure-ground” dimensions of transference: a selfobject dimension and representational configurations. On the basis of empirical studies of infancy, the paper proposes that both self- and mutual regulation organize the treatment relationship. It illustrates the clinical applicability of the expansion of Kohut's contributions by discussing a case in which countertransference, aggression, and resistance could place an analyst in danger of contributing to a therapeutic stalemate were it not for the contributions of Kohut.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction

Muriel Dimen PHD

Muriel Dimen

3 East 10th Street, Suite 1B1
New York, NY 10003

Muriel Dimen  

This symposium reconsiders sexuality from a relational perspective. It follows up on the Psychoanalytic Dialogues “Symposium on Gender” (Vol. 1, #3, 1991), which crystalized a fresh, deconstructive mode for thinking about gender that had emerged from nearly two decades of relational and feminist theorizing. This compilation initiates a new discussion. It is prompted by the critical, postclassical paradigm shift in which sexuality, once read as the driving force of desire, development, and pathology, has become that which is organized. To put it colloquially, if, with Freud, we thought that your sex is what makes you who you are, now we think that who you are shapes what sex you are and what sex you like. Not sexual drive and its conflicts, but the relational meaning of sexual desire, informs character and pathology, transference and countertransference. Clinical attention is less often on how the anatomical distinction between the sexes influences the psyche and more commonly on sexuality's relational complexity (see, e.g., Goldner, 1989; Mitchell, 1988), on how the psychological and cultural meaning of biology inscribes desire. Postclassical psychoanalysis thus implicitly destabilizes the nature and specificity of desire, treating them as emergents, not determinants.

This shift is manifest in the psychoanalytic reversal on sex and gender: in the classical version, sexuality organized gender; postclassical thinking, however, has it the other way around—gender organizes sexuality (Person, 1980). Although, then, the theory of psychosexuality preceded gender theory, the enormous recent interest in gender, gender identity, and gender development has obscured sexuality per se.

This symposium has benefited immeasurably from the advice of Lewis Aron and Sue A. Shapiro.

Dr. Dimen is Clinical Professor of Psychology, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University, and a Fellow at the New York Institute for the Humanities at New York University. She is also Faculty, Adelphi University, Derner Institute Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Subjectivity, Complexity and the Social World: Erikson's Identity Concept and Contemporary Relational Theories

Stephen Seligman DMH and Rebecca Shanok M.S.W.

Rebecca Shanok

3667 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

246 West End Avenue, #11C
New York, NY 10023

Rebecca Shanok  

Erik Erikson's concept of identity, reworked from the perspective of contemporary relational theory, is used to propose an integrative, dialectical view of subjectivity. This view captures dimensions of personal experience and personality organization that have stayed peripheral to most psychoanalytic discourse, from whatever point of view: Personal experience is created and re-created at the threshold of the intrapsychic and the social, such that they are inextricable, and this ubiquitous transaction is itself experienced as the ground and location of how it feels to be a person. Simultaneously, the vitality of personal experience rests on the dynamic synthesis of unity and complexity; this transactional perspective emerges amid the integration of diverse elements of personal experience, such as identity and intimacy, the past and the present, and the world of internal object representations and external relationships. Subjectivity is essentially constructed in the context of relationships; that is, it is intersubjective at its core. This implies extending the very definition of the relational to encompass the broadest view of the social environment, including families, institutions, cultural influences, and even broad historical forces.

The authors acknowledge with appreciation the advice and suggestions of Lewis Aron, Ph.D., Philip Aranow, Ed.D., Stanley Greenspan, M.D., and Calvin Settlage, M.D.

Dr. Seligman is Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at the Infant-Parent Program, San Francisco General Hospital-University of California, San Francisco. He is on the Faculty of the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California and is an Associate Member and Visiting Lecturer at the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

Dr. Shanok is Director of the Early Childhood Group Therapy Service and Training Programs of the Child Development Center, Jewish Board of Family and Children's Services, and is in private practice in New York City.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Essence of Kohut's Work and the Progress of Self Psychology

Howard Bacal MD

Howard Bacal

844 Norway Lane
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Howard Bacal  

This essay identifies Kohut's major contribution as methodological: that psychoanalytic inquiry entails the sustained empathic immersion in the patient's psychological experience. Kohut's consistent employment of this method enabled him to discover that it was not instinctual drive derivatives but selfobject needs that were central to all psychological relationships. This discovery was the basis for the transformation of analysts' approach to the “narcissistic” aspects of a wide variety of disorders—a transformation whose theoretical and therapeutic importance rivals the revolutionary approach taken by Freud to the vicissitudes of psychosexuality and its disturbances. The author describes the major areas of progress in self psychology—much of which centers on the growing recognition that the health and vitality of the self depend on complex relational, or intersubjective, selfobject experiences. He indicates how this recognition is changing our perspectives on transference and countertransference and is improving our ability to respond optimally to our patients. He describes how optimal responsiveness constitutes the guiding principle for therapeutic work, and how it may both constitute, and be different from, “being empathic.”

responseSet.title: Reply to Chodorow
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Chodorow

Ruth Stein PHD

Ruth Stein

25 Tchernichovski Street
Jerusalem 92578
Israel

Ruth Stein  

The serious attention and appreciation Nancy Chodorow has given my paper, for which I am deeply grateful, enables me to try to continue a dialogue with her, and with myself, that touches, to my mind, on some of the most pertinent problems facing us today. Not only is there the problem of retheorizing gender, but also the broader issue of the relations and clashes between clinical ways of working and understanding and contemporary cultural theorizing. I believe we need a double position, either in us or in the patient (I'm thinking of another analysand, who has been undergoing analysis while at the same time reflectively writing about cultural issues of gender), that will contain and embrace a dialectic relation between cultural (meta)theory and more immediate analytic experience. We require immediate clinical experience and broader cultural thought to help us mediate and decide among “intermediate” psychoanalytic theories. I say this at the beginning of my response to Chodorow because I notice that our discourse, both hers and mine, proceeds mainly on two levels: the immediate, particularized clinical level (affects, idiosyncratic sexual practices, style of speech, conscious and unconscious identifications, etc.), and the more abstract, metatheoretical level (different strands of feminism, issues of constructivism, and perhaps deconstructionism), where self-conscious and somewhat bewildered, we move, among different perspectives, in an effort to come to terms with this multiplicity. This is significant, I think, in that it reflects our lack of a one-theory support system.

Essentially, Chodorow argues, and tries to demonstrate through my case cum text, that one cannot generalize about gender, that there are traps where we fail to notice the cultural constructedness of gender, that we are uncertain with the present juxtaposition of conventional psychoanalytic knowledge and newer, so-called postanalytic, ideas, some of which are more in the nature of reflexive, subversive epistemologies than of alternative theories.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Theoretical Integration and Personal Commitment: Commentary on Seligman and Shanok

Neil Altman PHD

Neil Altman

127 West 79th Street, #3
New York, NY 10024

Neil Altman  

The theoretical and technical diversity that characterizes contemporary psychoanalysis makes many wonder about the extent to which integration is possible. Are all the various points of view now extant simply pieces of a larger puzzle? Are they partial, limited perspectives that, when properly integrated, will yield a comprehensive and unified theory covering all the bases? Or are the various points of view incompatible in certain respects, requiring analysts to choose among them? With respect to technique, different theories lead to divergent technical recommendations. Explicit self-disclosure by the analyst is an option, even a desirable option, for some relational analysts (e.g., Davies, 1994), whereas it is contraindicated for an analyst operating within a drive theory. Is there an overarching, integrative theory that can tell us when, or with which patients, a certain technical approach makes sense, and when, or with whom, it does not? Or, in the absence of such a theory, do technical choices require personal commitment and choice by an analyst faced with a multitude of theoretical frameworks within which to think about what to do?

In choosing to build their contemporary concept of identity on the foundation laid by Erik Erikson, Stephen Seligman and Rebecca Shahmoon Shanok are making an integrative effort.

Dr. Altman is faculty and supervisor at the Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis at New York University, and at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies. He is an Associate Editor of this journal.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Retaining Classical Concepts—Hidden Costs: Commentary on Lewis Aron's “The Internalized Primal Scene”

David Schwartz PHD

David Schwartz

786 Pinesbridge Road
Ossining, NY 10562

David Schwartz  

In “the internalized primal scene” Aron takes two related concepts from clinical theory, the combined parent figure and the primal scene and exposes them to rigorous analysis and modification. He argues for the validity and utility of these classical concepts with respect to a number of phenomena: creativity, grandiosity, splitting, obsessional defenses. Because he is interested in reducing what he sees as an exaggerated gap between preoedipal and oedipal phenomena in earlier theorizing, Aron goes on to develop links between the combined parent figure and the primal scene.

In its theorizing style, Aron's paper bears comparison with Benjamin's (1991), which attempted, in part, to show the value of the concept of penis envy when viewed from a particular developmental perspective. Both writers emphasize what they see as the value of retaining classical psychoanalytic concepts while redefining them and recasting them as metaphors, in the interest of developing a maximally rich theoretical vision. By indicating the ways in which the concepts he is arguing for do not necessarily entail the privileging of heterosexuality, procreation, or particular sexual acts, Aron's work is unique in anticipating potential feminist, gay, and epistemological critiques of his ideas.1

I would like to thank Gilbert Zicklin, whose editorial and conceptual collaboration was instrumental in the writing of this commentary.

David Schwartz is a psychoanalyst in private practice in Westchester and Manhattan. He is a member of the Psychoanalytic Society of the Westchester Center for the Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy.

1While Benjamin's (1991) work is clearly that of a feminist, she did not explicitly contend with the feminist critique of penis envy in the original presentation of her paper.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To Speak or Not To Speak? How and When Is That the Question?: Commentary on Papers by Davies and Gabbard**

Steven Knoblauch PHD

Steven Knoblauch

30 West 13th Street, #3A
New York, N.Y. 10011

Steven Knoblauch  

Erotic feelings transferential. countertransferential, or otherwise, can certainly be evocative. I found myself reacting quite strongly to the disagreement that Gabbard (1994) expressed with Davies's (1994a) use of self-disclosure of her erotic experience with her patient. Strongly, both because of my disagreement with Gabbard and because of my excitement over the invitation offered by these papers and the others in the symposium for those of us in the psychoanalytic community to converse about this topic. In the spirit of what I expect will be an ongoing conversation for some time to come, I share my perspective, as one voice and definitely not the final word.

Which brings me to my first point of disagreement with Gabbard's contribution, his tone. To say “we must draw the line at the brink of certain forms of self-disclosure” certainly sounds like a final word. It reminds me of Freud's injunction, pointed to by Tansey (1994), to “keep the countertransference in check” so that “we may not suddenly one day go further than we had intended” (Freud, 1915, p. 164). Gabbard's authoritative conclusion could be read as though we haven't come very far in 79 years, or that we have come full circle. But I think that Davies's brief review of the unfolding of psychoanalytic technique allows us to see how “a traditional understanding of analytic neutrality becomes untenable” (p. 156). She shows how we have come to recognize the inevitability of both analyst and analysand becoming enmeshed in the complicated reenactments that take place within the intersubjective field or space that an analytic pair unavoidably constitutes.

*Psychoanaltyic Dialogues 4(3), 1994.

Steven H. Knoblauch is in private practice in New York City and Hoboken, NJ.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Reply to Adam Phillips
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Adam Phillips

Judith Butler PHD

Judith Butler

Dept. of Rhetoric
University of California|Berkeley,
CA 94720

Judith Butler  

Adam Phillips's welcome commentary confirms that there might be a dialogue, even perhaps a psychoanalytic one, between a clinical and a speculative perspective on questions of gender, melancholia, and performativity. Clearly, the positions here are not as “staked out” as is often the case, for Phillips himself is both a clinician and a speculative thinker and, in this sense, furthers the kind of doubly dimensioned writing inaugurated by Freud. Indeed, what might at first seem like a strict opposition—the clinician on one hand and the cultural theorist of gender on the other—is broken down and reconfigured in the course of this exchange; and it is as much the content of his claims as the movement of his own thinking that makes me want to reconsider the oppositional framing and thinking that seems, luckily, not to be able to sustain itself here. My reply, then, will focus first on the question of whether melancholy is rightly understood to oppose or to temper the notion of gender performativity and, second, on whether sexual difference is itself an opposition that is as stable as it may appear.

Phillips suggests that the consideration of melancholic incorporation tempers the voluntarism of the position associated with gender performativity that emerged in the reception of Gender Trouble. On one hand, there appears to be a repudiated and unresolved knot of grief and, on the other, a self-conscious subject who, in Sartrian vein, creates itself anew again and again. But what if the terms of this opposition are not quite as stable as they seem? Consider that the irresolve of melancholia is tied to the check placed on aggression against the lost other, that the idealization of the other that accompanies the self-beratement in melancholia is precisely the routing of aggression against the ego that is prohibited from being expressed directly against that other.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Discussions of Oedipus and Beyond**

Jay Greenberg PHD

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, #1B
New York, NY 10024

Jay Greenberg  

I am grateful both for the thought that the reviewers of Oedipus and Beyond (Greenberg, 1991) have put into their papers and for the chance, given to me by the editors of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, to respond. The book itself is wide ranging, and so are the reviewers' comments. In a brief reply I can address only some of the issues that seem most pressing. In a similar vein, I cannot respond to Havens's (1995) paper, which, however much I appreciate what he has to say, does not directly address my main lines of argument. Despite these limitations, however, I think the reviews open a crucial conversation about some of the most important questions facing psychoanalysts today.

Hoffman's Discussion

The central vision underlying Hoffman's critique, as I understand it, is that my views are insufficiently anchored in a social-constructivist epistemology. In saying this about me, Hoffman (1995) is undoubtedly correct. But he also believes that I should want to be more anchored in constructivism. This is a point he argues weakly, when he bothers to argue it at all; it seems as self-evident to him that I should want to be a constructivist as it is to a true believer that membership in the One True Church is preferable to remaining a pagan.

*Psychoanalytic Dialogues 5 (1), 1995

Dr. Greenberg is Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Institute, and Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis, New York University. He is the author of Oedipus and Beyond (1991),

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Postmodern Impulse: Knowing and Being since Freud's Psychology by Barnaby Barratt

(Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 1993, xvi + 274 pp.)

Louis Sass PHD

Louis Sass

GSAPP-Busch Campus
Rutgers University|
Piscataway, NJ 08855-0819

Louis Sass  

Psychoanalysis, the richest and most influential of 20th-century psychologies, is also the one most fraught with fundamental ambiguities. Chief among these are the conflicting implications that the discovery, or (depending on one's point of view) the invention, of the unconscious holds for the possibility and value of human self-knowledge and self-mastery. Freud was fond of describing his psychoanalytic discoveries as a kind of latter-day Copernican revolution, which he saw as constituting a third great blow to human narcissism. Already displaced by Copernicus from a central position in the cosmos, already reminded by Darwin of his bestial origins, the human being was now being asked to acknowledge that even his consciousness, even reason itself, could not be master in its own house but instead was the plaything of forces grounded in instinct and the body, forces of which reason was almost wholly unaware.

The discovery of the unconscious can also be experienced rather differently, however, as is apparent from momentary reflection on Freud's comparison of the psychoanalytic with the Copernican and Darwinian revolutions.

Louis A. Sass is Professor of Clinical Psychology at Rutgers University. He is the author of Madness and Modernism: Insanity in the Light of Modern Art, Literature, and Thought (1992; pbk. 1994) and The Paradoxes of Delusion: Wittgenstein, Schreber, and the Schizophrenic Mind (1994). He is also coeditor of Hermeneutics and Psychological Theory (1988).

responseSet.title: Reply to Barnaby Barratt
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Barnaby Barratt

Louis Sass PHD

Louis Sass

Louis Sass  

Iwould first like to thank Barnaby Barratt for the many kind words he said about Madness and Modernism. I am gratified that he found the core of my book—the portrait of schizophrenic existence and the parallels drawn between this kind of experience and certain aspects of the art, literature, and philosophy of the last 100 years—to be compelling and worthwhile. The book is a hermeneutic study, an exercise in comparative phenomenology: I argue that a great many features of schizophrenia can best be understood as manifesting not the regression or dementia traditionally postulated, but various kinds of alienation and hyper-self-consciousness. As Barratt acknowledges, his criticisms are largely directed to issues that are treated at the end of Madness and Modernism and are somewhat peripheral to its central phenomenological themes—namely, issues of explanation (the etiological question of the possible social causation of schizophrenia) and of evaluation (my assessment of certain postmodernist figures). He also criticizes what he sees as my acceptance of three traditional distinctions: between culture and psyche, rational and irrational, and normal and abnormal. Given the overall tenor of Barratt's generous and appreciative review, it feels slightly churlish to be drafting this reply; nevertheless I shall endeavor to answer his criticisms and reservations.

Let me first address Barratt's criticism of me for distinguishing too sharply between culture and psyche. As a hermeneutically inclined scholar, I certainly agree that the two are interdependent, indeed in certain respects inseparable, as two aspects of the same, mutually interpenetrating human reality (as I thought I had made clear enough in my book). But this does not mean that it is not possible, indeed at times absolutely necessary, to separate them for analytic purposes. In my epilogue I am addressing the widespread medical-psychiatric view that schizophrenia is in all significant respects a biological illness, that social or cultural factors play no part in its etiology.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Schwartz

Lewis Aron PHD

Lewis Aron

243 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10023

Lewis Aron  

Michel Foucault (1979) describes how in the course of the 19th century a new type of author arose to whom he refers as “founders of discursivity…. They are unique in that they are not just the authors of their own works. They have produced something else: the possibilities and the rules for the formation of other texts.” Freud and Marx, whom Foucault considers the first and most important of these founders of discursivity, have both

established an endless possibility of discourse…. They have created a possibility for something other than their discourse, yet something belonging to what they founded…. Freud made possible a certain number of divergences—with respect to his own texts, concepts, and hypotheses—that all arise from the psychoanalytic discourse itself [pp. 114-115].

David Schwartz's argument with my paper strikes me as clear, strong, and provocative. Part of what makes his rhetoric so persuasive, however, is that he takes one side of the issue and sticks to it unrelentingly. This has the advantage that his argument seems simple and straightforward, and even morally and ethically compelling. Schwartz suggests that we simply abandon old problematic concepts and theories and move on without being weighed down by old Freudian baggage. I suggest, however, that the problems that Schwartz and I are struggling with, each in our own way, are far from simple or easily resolved. Schwartz's solution seems unsatisfactory to me precisely because (as he acknowledges in his response) it does lead to premature closure and the discarding of valuable ideas.

Schwartz seems to pride himself on his lack of ambivalence and lack of conflictedness, at least when it comes to the task of theoretical revision.

responseSet.title: Foreword
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Foreword

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell  

Self psychology has been among the most influential schools of psychoanalytic thought over the past two decades. The history of self psychology is naturally divisible into two phases: the first decade, from the publication of Kohut's Analysis of the Self in 1971 to Kohut's death in 1981, and the period since. Kohut had collaborators during his lifetime, but they were all clearly his students and worked within his vision. And Kohut's influence, like Freud's, has extended beyond his own lifespan. Like Freudian theory and Kleinian theory, self psychology was initially the creation of a single individual who cast an enormous shadow over the development of that theory after his death. The impact of Kohut, both his ideas and his personality, on the subsequent history of self psychology cannot be overestimated. As Philip Cushman (1995) noted in his recent history and critique of the development of psychotherapy in America, Kohut

was one of those rare, charismatic geniuses so attuned to the age that they speak and the rest of us recognize ourselves in their words. What he spoke about reorganized, or perhaps revolutionized, psychotherapeutic thinking and practice in the last quarter of the century. What he spoke about also related to aspects of the cultural frame of reference as diverse as popular culture, political campaigns, fine literature, and religious cults [pp. 320-321].

What has happened within self psychology since Kohut's death? A single voice has been replaced by a multiplicity of voices in complex relationships to each other. Are current trends within post-Kohut self psychology simply extensions of Kohut's vision? Are they wholly compatible with each other? Is current self psychology a singular psychology, or are there now several different self psychologies?

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Reply to Bromberg
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Bromberg

Peter Goldberg PHD

Peter Goldberg

1562 Oakview Avenue
Kensington, CA 94707

Peter Goldberg  

Dr. Bromberg's observations of the clinical phenomena of dissociation, and his understanding of the therapeutic task imposed by these clinical facts, converge extensively with my own experience and understanding of dissociation. This is so despite some obvious theoretical—and, to some degree, epistemological—differences between his approach and mine. Bromberg's work encourages me in the belief that progress is being made toward a psychoanalytic theory of dissociation as a distinct type of organization of psychical life. But what of the differing theoretical approaches to this phenomenon?

Bromberg himself does not enter into a speculation about the theoretical difference but illuminates the discrepancy in a detailed critique of my use of two terms—“pseudointegration” and “inauthenticity”—that I employ to conceptualize what I see as central properties of the dissociative process. What, then, is the discrepancy?

Bromberg views the conceptions of pseudointegration and inauthenticity as ill fitting or even antithetical to a relational, polyvocal view of self-formation—ill fitting because these terms seem to imply the existence of a fixed, positivistic conception of self-integration. Bromberg's approach reflects more completely the impact of a post-modern view of the individual, in which psychic reality is naturally in flux, its truth not fixed but contingent on the prevailing interpersonal and social construction of reality. Bromberg, not tethered by any notion of normative wholeness, is able to conceive of dissociation as the basic organization of self-experience, a normal and everpresent substratum of personality organization. One could perhaps say that dissociation reflects the natural pluralism of psychical experience. His view is both bold and comprehensive and in many ways far surpasses my own view in scope and elaboration.

Dissociation, in Bromberg's approach, refers to the coexistence of alternative ego states, ever shifting in relation to one another.

Oedipus and Beyond: A Clinical Theory by Jay Greenberg

(Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1991, x + 274 pp.)

Morris Eagle PHD

Morris Eagle

Department of Applied Psychology
Ontario Institute for Studies in Education|252
Bloor Street West
Toronto, Ontario M5S IV6
Canada

Morris Eagle  

I found Greenberg's book stimulating and thought provoking. As I read it, at virtually every page or two I found myself stopping to think through an issue raised or to register a particularly insightful and enlightening vignette. Often I ended up disagreeing with Greenberg's view. There is little doubt, however, that he raises issues that are fundamental to psychoanalysis. I will comment on some of these issues.

Drives

Greenberg takes the position—with which I agree—and argues cogently that Freud's dual-instinct theory (libido and aggression) is no longer (if it ever was) an adequate framework for psychoanalysis. Greenberg assumes, however, that a drive theory of some kind is necessary for any clinically meaningful psychoanalytic framework (and probably for any meaningful view of human behavior and human nature). This assumption is based on the further assumptions that, in order to account for human behavior, one must posit “endogenous forces at work within us that operate as prime movers of the mind” and recognize that “all mental activity begins with the stirring of some internal need” (p. 23).

Dr. Eagle recently completed a year as the Erikson Scholar in Residence at the Austen Riggs Center in Stockbridge, Massachusetts. He is coeditor, with James Barron and David Wolitsky, of the Interface of Psychoanalysis and Psychology (1992).

Afterword: A View from the Outside

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell  

The struggle among these authors to define the essential features of Kohut's contributions and the future direction for self psychology is of interest not only to proponents of self psychology, but also to anyone interested in the history of ideas, psychoanalytic and otherwise.

All these authors note the richness of Kohut's innovations in both method (sustained empathic immersion in the patient's subjective reality) and theory (particularly the selfobject concept). Yet, Stolorow argues, there seem to be two groups of theorists in self psychology today: the loyalists and the expansionists. These terms are heavily value laden and, naturally enough, reflect Stolorow's own particular vantage point. Another way to characterize these groupings would be to contrast their strategies of theorizing and the way they position Kohut's contribution vis-à-vis other psychoanalytic schools.

One strategy (including Basch's and the Ornsteins') establishes Kohut's work as constituting a complete, enduring, revolutionary paradigm, leaving only small details or implications to be worked out.

A second strategy positions Kohut's ouvre as a transitional development on the way to the fuller emergence of a more encompassing, more comprehensively revolutionary paradigm. Thus, Stolorow regards intersubjectivity theory and relational theories of various sorts as providing a more complete field or systems model, emphasizing the fully contextual interaction of subjectivities with reciprocal, mutual influence. Stolorow positions Kohut as caught between paradigms and regards as anachronistic some of Kohut's basic concepts, like the reification of a self with a nuclear program or inherent design.

In a different, but closely related, fashion, Bacal also regards Kohut's self psychology as incomplete and transitional. Bacal positions self psychology as half a (relational)

responseSet.title: Reply to Louis A. Sass
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Louis A. Sass

Barnaby Barratt PHD

Barnaby Barratt

Barnaby Barratt  

It is a privilege to have one's work well read, a pleasure to have it well regarded, and a rare honor to have it thoughtfully criticized. I do know how fortunate I am that my Psychoanalysis and the Postmodern Impulse has engaged the appreciative but not uncritical attention of a scholar of Professor Sass's capabilities. In a few pages he has deftly summarized for the reader of Psychoanalytic Dialogues many of the coordinates of my recent thinking about psychoanalytic discourse, and I trust it will not seem merely quarrelsome if this rejoinder now focuses on the significance of those coordinates he omits and why, from my point of view, the flaws in his hermeneutic stance are illuminated by these omissions. Sass's review is, I believe, successful in indicating the ways in which he and I part company, and for that I thank him. I believe his review is less successful in its grasp of why we part company and the implications of this divergence.

If this brief exchange between Sass and me were to be extended, it might usefully explore, in the context of the problematics of psychoanalytic inquiry, some of the profoundest dilemmas in which the western intellectual tradition is currently immersed. Although Sass's review does not address this aspect of my book directly, I believe I could convince him of one of the major theses therein, namely, that a revolution in thinking has been set in motion by the transitions of this century, that it is a revolution that we can scarcely comprehend because we are in its midst, and that the history of psychoanalysis has operated both as an instigator of this revolution and as a reaction against it.

Philosophically, this “revolution” has been labeled “the end of metaphysics,” but perhaps it is most easily appreciated as the loss of a transcendental or foundational point of universal reference by which the diversities of thinking and communication, the plurivocality of human experiences and values, might be commonly anchored or organized as a unity.

responseSet.title: “Self Psychologists” Everywhere??: Response to Virginia Goldner
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Self Psychologists” Everywhere??: Response to Virginia Goldner**

John Lindon M.D., Ph.D.

John Lindon

255 S. Beverly Glen Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90024

John Lindon  

Understandably Virginia Goldner is furious with me. she states, “yet Lindon derisively characterizes may request for clarity as an obsessional preoccupation appropriate to ‘debates about who is buried in Grant's Tomb’” (p. 312). Yet when I read my reply to her commentary, I find that I wrote:

I disagree with Goldner and Flax (1993) that “truth is discourse specific….” That notion may apply if we are debating who is buried in Grant's Tomb, but when we come to what the patient is experiencing, it is the patient who is the absolute arbiter. As Schwaber (1990) states, “Real is what each of us experiences as true—the correctness of which no other one of us can be the arbiter” (p. 232) [Lindon, 1994b, p. 627].

I am sorry—and astonished—that Goldner experienced that paragraph as derision or that I was in some way calling her obsessional; I certainly do not experience it that way, nor do I see how she arrived at that idea. And that is precisely the point I was trying to make: that her experience is hers; she experienced it as real and true, “the correctness of which no other one of us can be the arbiter.” Now, knowing how she experienced what I wrote, I can understand the strident tone in her polemic and her rage at me for what she experienced as my real denigration of her personally.

*Psychoanalytic Dialogues 5(2), 1995.

Dr. Lindon is a Faculty Member, Supervising and Training Analyst, and Founding Member, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles, California; Faculty Member, Supervising and Training Analyst, and Past President, Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute; and Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA School of Medicine.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Madness and Modernism: Insanity in the Light of Modern Art, Literature, and Thought by Louis A. Sass

(New York: Basic Books, 1992, xii + 595 pp.)

Barnaby Barratt PHD

Barnaby Barratt

7480 Greenwich East
Bloomfield, MI 48301-3920

Barnaby Barratt  

There is little doubt that this volume deserves to become a landmark in the scholarly study of insanity. The sheer breadth of its scope and the extensiveness of its documentation render it an indispensable reference for students and constitute an achievement of scholarship that should make the volume required for serious reading and contemplation by all those interested in understanding the experiences of insanity. Indeed, Madness and Modernism, which is both a tenaciously presented argument and a richly rewarding compendium on insanity in relation to recent cultural developments, is a treasure that almost buries itself beneath the weightiness of its extraordinary erudition. This is a landmark that risks underappreciation. So I intend here to show why the book is so valuable, and then—because the invitation for me to write this review was issued on the basis of my holding some rather divergent views of the issues expounded in it—I shall raise some questions about certain aspects of Sass's thesis.

In so many ways, what Sass sets out to do, he does superbly. He asks whether madness might be “not an escape from but an exacerbation of that thoroughgoing illness” that Dostoevski described as too much consciousness—what if it were to derive from “a heightening rather than a dimming of conscious awareness” (p. 4).

Barnaby B. Barratt, Ph.D. is in full-time psychoanalytic practice in the greater Detroit area. He is on the faculty of the Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute and is Associate Professor of Family Practice, Psychiatry and Behavioral Neurosciences, Wayne State University, where he is also a Visiting Professor in the Department of English.

[[RunningHead]]

Self Psychology: Later, The Same Day Response to Editors' Follow-Up Questions

Frank Lachmann PHD and Beatrice Beebe PHD

Beatrice Beebe

393 West End Avenue, #1-A
New York, NY 10024

722 West 168th Street, #108
New York, NY 10032

Beatrice Beebe  

There is a remarkable diversity of ideas among the contributing analysts, who nevertheless agree about the essentials of self psychology. In our view, the differences among the authors are based on the extent of the authors' relationship to Heinz Kohut, the diversity of prior training and philosophical beliefs, and varying desires to stretch self psychology to unexplored areas.

The selfobject concept is used in many different ways in the self psychology literature. The experiences that provide selfobject functlons can range from reciprocated love relations, a chess game, a work of art, music, a beautiful day, to an addiction to heroin, food, or pain. We regard the selfobject experience as an attempt at self-regulation through self-strengthening, enlivening, and affect regulation, even if the means seem self-destructive. At best, this attempt contributes to affective vitalization, self-coherence, and a sense of continuity in time.

We do not see the selfobject dimension of the transference in contrast to another type of object but in a figure—ground relationship to the representational configurations (see Stolorow and Lachmann, 1980; Lichtenberg, 1983; Lachmann and Beebe, 1992). The two dimensions serve different functions, but each is a critical context for organizing and interpreting the other. If we confine ourselves to self-object transferences, we may neglect the themes that organize the patient's representation of past and current experiences. If we confine ourselves to the interpretation of representational configurations, we may lose our sensitivity to the patient's vulnerabilities and strivings for self-restoration.

The representational configuration can be illustrated by a patient who formed a dependent, self-depreciating relationship with her analyst.

responseSet.title: Follow-Up Questions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Follow-Up Questions

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell  

After we received the initial set of answers we asked the following additional questions:

In addition to what you all consider to be Kohut's revolutionary methodological innovation in his contributions on “empathy,” the concept of the “selfobject” (-need, -experience, -transference) is regarded as his central theoretical innovation. Yet each of you uses the term selfobject in your own distinct and particular way. Perhaps part of the confusion is due to the tendency to define selfobjects by distinguishing them from Freud's instinctual objects. Historically, of course, it was precisely through this distinction between objects of sexual and aggressive drives and objects that function to preserve narcissistic equilibrium that Kohut (1971) gave birth to the selfobject concept. For many contemporary clinicians and theorists, however, drive theory metapsychology as a whole is no longer particularly useful or interesting. As the drive theory framework fades, how are we now to understand the selfobject experience in contrast to the terms in which it was defined? In a postdrive-theory conceptual field, how is selfobject experience to be understood? Selfobjects in contrast to what other sorts of objects?

Let us pose the question another way: Was the selfobject concept a device through which human relations in general were redefined and differently understood so that now all the significant relationships (in which the individual is inevitably embedded) are to be construed as serving selfobject needs? Or are selfobject needs best construed as specific and distinct from other important kinds of relational needs and dimensions of human relatedness (not just the need for sexual and aggressive discharge à la drive theory)?

How is the regeneration of painful experience best understood? Concepts like “representational” or “repetitive” dimensions of the transference and “bad selfobjects” are efforts to struggle with this problem.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

Loyalism and Expansionism in Self Psychology Response to Editors' Follow-Up Questions

Robert Stolorow PHD

Robert Stolorow

11726 San Vicente Boulevard, #410
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Robert Stolorow  

In considering the editors' questions against the backdrop of the position papers collected in this issue, I have found it useful to divide the various current contributors to the self psychology literature into two broad groups. I call them the loyalists and the expansionists. The loyalists tend to be members of Kohut's original inner circle of former analysands and students who experienced close personal relationships with him. Most of the expansionists, by contrast, know Kohut's work from more of a distance, perhaps primarily from his writings. Correspondingly, whereas most of the loyalists reside in Chicago or nearby Cincinnati, expansionists are drawn from all over North America—from New York City, New Jersey, Boston, Washington, DC, Los Angeles, Toronto, and Vancouver.

Whereas expansionists, who also tend to be integrationists, wish to bring into self psychology ideas from sources other than Kohut, loyalists, in the words of Lachmann and Beebe (this issue), adhere to “the assumption that Kohut's writings constitute the authoritative statement.” For loyalists, Kohut's word is the first and the last, and they thus tend to be openly dismissive of the contributions of the expansionists. The Ornsteins (this issue), for example, write that post-Kohut self psychology has achieved “a few relatively minor corrections … but cannot claim significant advances in new conceptualizations.” The Ornsteins regard attempts to delineate selfobject transferences other than Kohut's original trilogy as only “marginally justified.” Paul Ornstein (1994, personal communication) has even gone so far as to declare that people are interested in the theory of intersubjectivity mostly because they “do not know and do not read and do not fully understand Kohut's writings.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interaction in Psychoanalysis A Broadening Horizon

James Fosshage PHD

James Fosshage

330 West 58th Street, Suite 200
New York, NY 10019

James Fosshage  

To view all that occurs in the analytic arena, including verbal exchange, as interaction broadens the horizon and affects our understanding of the analytic process, transference-countertransference, therapeutic action, and theory of technique. This paper focuses particularly on implications of the organization model of transference: the process of illuminating transference, the mutual interactional shaping that occurs in the analytic relationship, and the distinction between the content and the process of communications. Defining countertransference as the analyst's experience of the patient, the paper proposes that all analysts, regardless of persuasion, use their experience of the patient (that is, countertransference) as the central guide to analytic exploration and understanding. Differences in analysts' experience of their patients arise not only because of different models and respective subjectivities, but also because of the variable use of two principal listening perspectives: listening from within the patient's vantage point (subject-centered listening perspective) and listening from the as-the-other (other-centered) vantage point. The paper proposes that the analyst's listening from within and as-the-other, oscillating in a background-foreground configuration, can illuminate more fully the patient's experience of self and of self in relation to others. And, finally, recognizing that all interventions are interactions firmly anchors the interpretive process and its power within relational experience and opens the door to reassessing and potentially embracing the many varied forms of interventions that contribute to the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis.

James L. Fosshage, Ph.D. is a Board Director and faculty member of the National Institute for the Psychotherapies, NYC; a member of the Core Faculty, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, NYC; and Faculty member and Supervisor, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University.

Portions of this paper were presented at the Annual Conference of the National Institute for the Psychotherapies on “Interaction in Psychoanalysis,” New York City, April 24, 1993, and at the Spring meeting of the Division of Psychoanalysis of the American Psychological Association in Washington, DC, April 16, 1994.
