[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Dissociation, and Personality Organization Reflections on Peter Goldberg's Essay

Philip Bromberg PHD

Philip Bromberg

300 Central Park West, #7C
New York, NY 10024

Philip Bromberg  

If one wished to read the contemporary psychoanalytic literature as a serialized Gothic romance, it is not hard to envision the restless ghost of Pierre Janet, banished from the castle by Sigmund Freud a century ago, returning for an overdue haunting of Freud's current descendants. With uncanny commonality, most major schools of analytic thought have become appropriately more responsive to the phenomenon of dissociation, and each in its own way is attempting actively to accommodate it within its model of the mind and its approach to clinical process. A pivotal concept in the birth and development of interpersonal psychoanalysis (Sullivan, 1940, 1953) and “independent” British object relational theories (Winnicott, 1945, 1949, 1960, 1971a; Fairbairn, 1944, 1952), dissociation continues to receive its most active clinical and theoretical attention from contemporary analysts whose sensibilities most directly represent one or both of these schools of thought (e.g., Bromberg, 1984, 1991, 1993b, 1994; Smith, 1989; Mitchell, 1991, 1993; Davies and Frawley, 1992, 1994; Harris, 1992, 1994; Reis, 1993; Schwartz, 1994). It has also found its way into the work of analysts writing from a self-psychological orientation, particularly those interested in the phenomenology of self-states (e.g., Stolorow, Brandchaft, and Atwood, 1987; Ferguson, 1990) and has gained stature among Freudian analysts, both classical (e.g., Shengold, 1989, 1992; Kernberg, 1991; Brenner, 1994), and postclassical (e.g., Marmer, 1980, 1991; Goldberg, 1987; Gabbard, 1992; Lyon, 1992; Roth, 1992).

Dr. Bromberg is Training and Supervising Analyst, W. A. White Psychoanalytic Institute; and Clinical Professor of Psychology and Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Keeping It Moving: Commentary on Judith Butler's “Melancholy Gender—Refused Identification”

Adam Phillips

Adam Phillips

99 Talbot Road
London W11
England

Adam Phillips  

Ends of sentences and other pauses only come when we run out of time or hope.

Carolyn Creedon, The Best American Poetry

If, as Freud (1923) suggests, character is constituted by identification—the ego likening itself to what it once loved—then character is close to caricature, an imitation of an imitation. Like the artists Plato wanted to ban, we are making copies of copies, but unlike Plato's artists we have no original, only an infinite succession of likenesses to someone who, to all intents and purposes, does not exist. Freud's notion of character is a parody of a Platonic work of art; his theory of character formation through identification makes a mockery of character as in any way substantive. The ego is always dressing up for somewhere to go. Insofar as being is being like, there can be no place for True selves or core gender identities. After all, my sense of authenticity can come only from the senses of authenticity in my culture. In this context, my True Self is more accurately described as my Preferred Self (or Selves). I am the performer of my conscious and unconscious preferences.

Lacan's mirror-stage is a testament to the havoc wreaked by mimetic forms of development; and Mikkel Borch-Jacobsen (1993) and Leo Bersani (1986, 1990) in particular have exposed the violence and tautology of Freud's theory of identification, the mutual implication and complicity involved in being like.

Adam Phillips is Principal Child Psychotherapist at the Wolverton Gardens Child and Family Consultation Centre in London.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Oedipus and Beyond: A Clinical Theory by Jay Greenberg

(Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1991, x + 274 pp.)

Irwin Hoffman PHD

Irwin Hoffman

55 E. Washington Street, Suite 1217
Chicago, IL 60602

Irwin Hoffman  

The Baby and the Bathwater

It's impossible for a critique of any serious book, let alone one as clinically and theoretically rich as Oedipus and Beyond, to be exhaustive or to do justice to all its contents and potential implications. In fact, any review is bound to be a selective construction, one that highlights certain aspects while underemphasizing or completely ignoring others that undoubtedly deserve attention. Greenberg's ideas are quite complex, moreover, and not easy to classify. In regard to Pine's (1990) four major types of theory, for example, is Greenberg a drive theorist? An ego-psychological theorist? A self-psychological theorist? Or an object-relational theorist? All of the above? None of the above? To grasp what Greenberg is trying to say you have to attend carefully, and you have to work at it. You can't assume you've got it because of one or another buzzword or phrase that you can assimilate neatly into some familiar theoretical schema.

The complexity of Greenberg's theorizing, however, should not prevent our trying to search out its possible weaknesses.

Irwin Z. Hoffman is a Lecturer in Psychiatry at the University of Illinois Medical School at Chicago, faculty and supervising analyst at the Chicago Center for Psychoanalysis, and faculty and supervising analyst at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

A version of this critique was originally presented at the conference “What Drives the Mind? Jay Greenberg: A Revised Clinical Theory,” sponsored by the Colorado Center for Psychoanalytic Studies and the Colorado Society for Psychology and Psychoanalysis, Denver, Colorado, June 6, 1992.

© 1995 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Patient's Dreams and the Countertransference

Mark Blechner PHD

Mark Blechner

145 Central Park West
New York, NY 10023

Mark Blechner  

Dreams reported in psychoanalysis may reflect not only the transference of the patient, but also the countertransference, counterresistance, and counteranxiety of the psychoanalyst. Because of the communicative capacity of dreams, the analyst may use the patient's dreams as supervision. Objective signs are described that may indicate countertransference interference in the analyst's dream interpretation. Through a process of reciprocal, interactive dream interpretation, the dream's meaning about the transference-countertransference matrix can be clarified. Usually, there is an intermediate stage in working on a dream, during which the dream meaning is enacted in the dream-interpretation process. Clinical examples include the “Lovely Dream” reported by Freud, along with examples from the casebook of Gill and Hoffman (1982) and from the author's own practice.

Cincinnati, OH 45229

Paul Ornstein  

The greatest divergence among us appears to be in our use of the concept selfobject clinically and theoretically. The concept of transference is used in different ways by the contributors, and this may explain the difference in the conceptualization of how unconscious aspects of past experiences enter the treatment process.

In relation to the concept of the selfobject, since we have moved beyond drive theory (as we all say we have), the “object”—which was always a “drive-object” (invested with libido and aggression)—also has to be left behind. The concept has too much theoretical baggage attached to it. We need a different conceptual term for the “other” in self psychology. Initially, the archaic selfobject1 (with its key function of providing structure building experiences) was heuristically felicitous with all of its varied implications—a usefully elastic term. It was also helpful to contrast the archaic selfobject with the object of traditional analysis, but this is a historical artifact. Kohut (1977, 1980) later corrected this when he contrasted the archaic selfobject with the mature selfobject. He retained the concept of the object clinically (when the experience was of a sexual or aggressive nature) but without having a chance later on for an adequate theoretical elaboration (Kohut, 1984).

We believe this question comes up repeatedly because—as Bacal indicated—many in the field retain the idea of a continuum from merger to distinctness and separateness and link selfobject experiences to degrees of separateness.

1Although Kohut consistently used the term selfobject, he clearly meant what we now describe as selfobject experiences—thus our current usage does not represent conceptual innovation but only a linguistic improvement and clarification.

Howard Bacal  

In my responses to your initial questions, I addressed much of what these further questions comprise. I believe you are right when you say that “for many contemporary clinicians and theorists, … drive theory metapsychology as a whole is no longer particularly useful or interesting.” Self psychology has not redefined human relationships through the concept of the selfobject; it avers that selfobject experience has always been at the core of the psychological aspects of human relationships. Are all significant relationships to be construed as serving selfobject needs? I can only answer from the perspective of my own clinical experience. While employing the methodology that characterizes the self-psychological approach (as I have described it), I have not seen any patients whose psychological relationships with significant others do not centrally entail selfobject experience or the need for selfobject responsiveness. But as I indicated earlier, self psychologists have paid insufficient attention to the experience of providing selfobject functioning. To the extent that one experiences oneself as providing it, this may also evoke and vitalize the sense of self of both analyst and analysand. In this sense as well, the selfobject concept is truly relational: the experience of providing selfobject responsiveness as well as the experience of its provision. Lachmann and Beebe and Stolorow imply this in their answers as well. In other words, selfobject need not only entails the need to receive from the other; it also very much includes the need to give, as Suttie (1935) indicated, to share, and to enter into reciprocal relatedness. All these experiences evoke, affirm, or enhance the sense of self.

An Interview with Otto Kernberg

Over the past 20 years, Otto Kernberg has been one of the key figures responsible for the widespread, burgeoning interest in object relations theories. Kernberg's methodology for innovation has always been conservative (in the best sense of the term) and integrative. While maintaining a deep respect for Freudian tradition, he has introduced many very important shifts in thinking about transference, countertransference, psychic structure, diagnosis, analytic technique, the nature of (and capacity for) love, sexuality, aggression, the hospital milieu, psychoanalytic education, narcissism, and much more. While firmly grounded in Freudian ego psychology, he helped integrate contributions from Kleinian theory and make them accessible to American readers. While deeply committed to drive theory, he helped popularize Fairbairn's relational notions of unconscious structures. While his contributions have always been controversial and certainly have not attained a consensus, he has been a major force to be reckoned with by any contemporary psychoanalytic theoretician.

PD: If we consider the span of nearly two decades from the publication of Borderline Conditions and Pathological Narcissism to today, do you feel there have been any basic changes or shifts in emphasis in your thinking, either in theory and in terms of clinical practice?

The contemporary quandary about the grounding of meaning has forced us to direct our investigative gaze onto ourselves as participants (as clinicians) in the therapeutic encounter as well as to preserve the more traditional focus on our patients. Moreover, the current recognition of the therapist's participant role in the therapeutic process—whether given a minimalist construal in terms of selective listening, the collecting of partial data, or the imposition of presumptive interpretive models on the patient's material; or a more full-blown construal in terms of the role of the therapist's conscious and unconscious communications in shaping the ongoing interaction between patient and therapist—has made us all aware of the epistemological, and ultimately moral, issues that dog every step of our work. In particular, how do we understand and represent the experience of the Other: in the case at hand, the life-world of our patients, their experiences, their unconscious fantasies, their concerns, their “hopes and dreads” (Mitchell, 1993). To be sure, a similar problem inheres in various social science disciplines, most notably anthropology, which has struggled quite self-consciously with the problem of representing difference (Clifford, 1988). In psychoanalysis, however, the problem becomes particularly tricky given the presumption of unconscious meanings that are not straightforwardly accessible to the patient and the task of uncovering these meanings while avoiding reductive interpretations. Moreover, just as anthropology's problem has become more urgent by the recent participation of third-world intellectuals on the academic scene, each claiming the prerogative to speak their own culture (Cesaire, 1983), analogously, the commitments of the members in various self-help/self-advocacy groups—consciousness raising, 12-step, different survivor groups—revolve around a similar insistence on having exclusive priority in representing themselves: that is, telling

At this time in the evolution of the various psychoanalytic theories, we can make only some general comments regarding the direction that psychoanalytic self psychology may take in the future. We can, with greater certainty, however, predict the enduring importance of two of its basic concepts: 1) the significance of empathy as a mode of observation will endure; and 2) the developmental and clinical conception of the selfobject will remain central to self psychology for the foreseeable future. Selfobject experiences have been recognized as crucial in all aspects of mental life: in development, in the clinical situation, and in everyday life throughout the lifespan.

“I don't know”: Resistance or Groping for Words?: The Construction of Analytic Subjectivity

Julie Gerhardt PHD and Charles Stinson MD

Charles Stinson

763 Ashbury Street
San Francisco, CA 94117

401 Parnassus Avenue
San Francisco, CA 94133

Charles Stinson  

In poststructuralist thought, in particular ethnographically oriented studies of culture, language, action, or text, context has become an indispensable concept as a way of challenging the claims of an autonomous meaning-making subject with the corresponding depiction of the mind as an encapsulated formal representational system that can be detached from the various strata of social organization. Instead, context is recognized as providing a frame of interpretation for the production of meaning in terms of which events embedded within the frame must be understood. In psychoanalytic theorizing, the recent shift from a one-person to a two-person paradigm can similarly be characterized in terms of an appeal to context. Whereas invocations of the relevance of context in determining the patient's subjective experience in the analytically based process typically lead to a concern with the therapist's subjectivity or countertransference, thus promoting the view of the clinical process in terms of the mutually reciprocal influence of the patient and therapist, in contrast, the present paper focuses on another

Julie Gerhardt is Associate Professor of Psychology at the California Institute of Integral Studies in San Francisco. She is also a member of the Northern California Society for Psychoanalytic Psychology.

Charles Stinson is Codirector of the Program on Conscious and Unconscious Mental Processes of the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation's Program on Mental Health and Human Development. He is Associate Professor of Psychiatry at the University of California, San Francisco.

dimension of the context in analytically based work, namely, the particular set of the theoretical assumptions that constitute the therapist's professionally grounded preconceptions about the nature of psychological distress. It is argued that these assumptions function as “demand characteristics” in that they structure the therapist's mode of intervention and thus, ultimately, how the therapeutic work is carried out. In particular, the argument is made that these demand characteristics play a role in bringing about a self-reflexive stance on the patient's part in which one part of the self observes and reflects on other parts of the self (thoughts, feelings, fantasies, etc.) and begins to cultivate the skills of self-expression and self-formulation as the key to understanding personal distress. We refer to this self-reflexive stance as one of “analytic subjectivity” and raise the question of how such attention to the self is brought about. Then, after having characterized this aspect of the contextual frame, in the second part of this paper we go on to examine a particular event that occurs within the frame. Specifically, we examine a patient's use of the discourse marker i don't know from the transcripts of a psychotherapy study and suggest that its use can be understood in terms of the context-driven agenda of having the patient self-inspect and self-reflect.
